perturbation.original_text: Evaluation Material . The term “Evaluation Material” shall be deemed to include all information, records, notes, analyses, compilations, studies, reports, projections, forecasts, interpretations or other documents prepared by each party or its Representatives that contain, reflect or are based upon, in whole or in part, the information furnished to such party or its Representatives pursuant hereto, which is not available to the general public.
perturbation.changed_text: Evaluation Material . The term “Evaluation Material” shall be deemed to include all information, records, notes, analyses, compilations, studies, reports, projections, forecasts, interpretations or other documents prepared by each party or its Representatives that contain, reflect or are based upon, in whole or in part, the information furnished to such party or its Representatives pursuant hereto, regardless of its availability to the general public.
perturbation.explanation: By stating that 'Evaluation Material' includes information regardless of its availability to the general public, the contract now conflicts with the established legal definition of 'trade secret,' which requires information to not be generally known or readily ascertainable to be protected. This expands the definition of 'Evaluation Material' beyond what could be considered a legitimate trade secret under the law.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text contradicts the DTSA's definition of 'trade secret,' which requires that the information not be generally known or readily ascertainable. The altered definition in the contract includes information regardless of its public availability, undermining the necessary element of secrecy for trade secret protection under federal law.

Definitions As used in this chapter- (1) the term "foreign instrumentality" means any agency, bureau, ministry, component, institution, association, or any legal, commercial, or business organization, corporation, firm, or entity that is substantially owned, controlled, sponsored, commanded, managed, or dominated by a foreign government; (2) the term "foreign agent" means any officer, employee, proxy, servant, delegate, or representative of
perturbation.justification: The changed text broadens the definition of 'Evaluation Material' to include information regardless of its availability to the general public. This contradicts the established legal definition of a 'trade secret,' which requires that the information not be generally known or readily ascertainable. 18 USC 1839, defines terms relevant to the protection of trade secrets, implicitly requiring secrecy as a component of a trade secret, a condition undermined by the new definition. The altered definition makes the contract unenforceable under trade secret law.

perturbation.original_text: The receiving party shall safeguard the Confidential Information with at least the same degree of care to avoid unauthorized disclosure and use as Recipient uses to protect its own confidential information, but in no event less than a reasonable standard of care.
perturbation.changed_text: The receiving party shall safeguard the Confidential Information with a minimal degree of care to avoid unauthorized disclosure and use, as determined by the receiving party's internal policies.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text removes the 'reasonable standard of care' requirement and replaces it with a subjective standard based on the receiving party's internal policies. This could allow for a lower level of protection than is legally required for safeguarding confidential information, particularly concerning data privacy laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: CCPA mandates reasonable security procedures and practices appropriate to the nature of the personal information to protect the personal information. By allowing a 'minimal degree of care' as determined by internal policies, the modified text could fall short of the 'reasonable security' requirement, violating CCPA's data protection standards for California residents' personal information.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA) | State of California - Department of Justice - Office of the Attorney General Skip to main content California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA) Home Privacy California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA) Updated on March 13, 2024 The California Consumer Privacy Act of 2018 (CCPA) gives consumers more control over the personal information that businesses collect about them and the CCPA regulations provide guidance on how to implement the law. This landmark law secures new privacy rights for California consumers, including: The right to know about the personal information a business collects about them and how it is used and shared; The right to delete personal information collected from them (with some exceptions); The right to opt-out of the sale or sharing of their personal information including via the GPC ; The right to non-discrimination for exercising their CCPA rights. In November of 2020, California voters approved Proposition 24, the CPRA , which amended the CCPA and added new additional privacy protections that began on January 1, 2023. As of January 1, 2023, consumers have new rights in addition to those above, such as: The right to correct inaccurate personal information that a business has about them; and The right to limit the use and disclosure of sensitive personal information collected about them. Businesses that are subject to the CCPA have several responsibilities, including responding to consumer requests to exercise these rights and giving consumers certain notices explaining their privacy practices . The CCPA applies to many businesses, including data brokers . CPRA amends the CCPA; it does not create a separate, new law. As a result, our office typically refers to the law as “CCPA” or “CCPA, as amended.” Links to Topics A. GENERAL INFORMATION ABOUT THE CCPA B. RIGHT TO OPT-OUT OF SALE OR SHARING C. REQUESTS TO KNOW D. REQUESTS TO DELETE E. REQUESTS TO CORRECT F. REQUESTS TO LIMIT USE OF PERSONAL INFORMATION G. RIGHT TO NON-DISCRIMINATION H. REQUIRED NOTICES I. DATA BROKERS AND THE CCPA Other Consumer Resources on CCPA Frequently Asked Questions (FAQs) These FAQs provide general consumer information about the CCPA and how you can exercise your rights under the CCPA. They are not legal advice, regulatory guidance, or an opinion of the Attorney General. We will update this information periodically. A. GENERAL INFORMATION ABOUT THE CCPA 1. What rights do I have under the CCPA? If you are a California resident, you may ask businesses to disclose what personal information they have about you and what they do with that information, to delete your personal information, to direct businesses not to sell or share your personal information, to correct inaccurate information that they have about you, and to limit businesses’ use and disclosure of your sensitive personal information: Right to know : You can request that a business disclose to you: (1) the categories and/or specific pieces of personal information they have collected about you, (2) the categories of sources for that personal information, (3) the purposes for which the business uses that information, (4) the categories of third parties with whom the business discloses the information, and (5) the categories of information that the business sells or discloses to third parties. You can make a request to know up to twice a year, free of charge. Right to delete : You can request that businesses delete personal information they collected from you and tell their service providers to do the same, subject to certain exceptions (such as if the business is legally required to keep the information). Right to opt-out of sale or sharing : You may request that businesses stop selling or sharing your personal information (“opt-out”), including via a user-enabled global privacy control. Businesses cannot sell or share your personal information after they receive your opt-out request unless you later authorize them to do so again. Right to correc
perturbation.justification: The modified text replaces the 'reasonable standard of care' with a 'minimal degree of care' based on internal policies. This directly contradicts the CCPA's mandate for 'reasonable security procedures and practices appropriate to the nature of the personal information to protect the personal information,' as mentioned in the Law Explanation and supported by Scraped Law Snippet 2, which details the CCPA and its aim to give consumers more control over their personal information. The original text ensured a baseline of 'reasonable standard of care,' but the modified text allows for potentially inadequate protection based solely on a company's internal assessment, thereby weakening the legal obligation to protect personal data.

perturbation.original_text: In the event that a party or its Representatives are required (by oral questions, interrogatories, requests for information or documents in legal proceedings, subpoena, civil investigative demand or other similar process or any rule, regulation or policy statement of any national securities exchange, market or automated quotation system on which any of the receiving party’s securities are listed or quoted) to disclose (i) any of the other party’s Evaluation Material or (ii) that discussions are taking place concerning the Transaction, the party requested or required to make the disclosure shall provide the other party with prompt notice of any such request or requirement so that the other party may seek a protective order or other appropriate remedy and/or waive compliance with the provisions of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event that a party or its Representatives are required to disclose any of the other party’s Evaluation Material, the party required to make the disclosure will make reasonable attempts to notify the other party.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause mandates 'prompt notice' allowing the other party time to seek a protective order. This modified clause weakens that requirement to 'reasonable attempts,' removing the obligation to ensure the other party has sufficient time to act, thus potentially violating legal discovery obligations.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Federal Rules of Civil Procedure, Rule 26
perturbation.law_explanation: FRCP 26 outlines the scope and process of discovery in federal lawsuits, including the obligation to respond completely and truthfully to discovery requests. The 'reasonable attempts' language is insufficient to satisfy the duty to cooperate in discovery and could obstruct the other party's ability to protect confidential information through a protective order, as outlined under Rule 26(c).
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Rule 26. Duty to Disclose; General Provisions Governing Discovery | Federal Rules of Civil Procedure | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Rule 26. Duty to Disclose; General Provisions Governing Discovery (a) Required Disclosures. (1) Initial Disclosure. (A) In General. Except as exempted by Rule 26(a)(1)(B) or as otherwise stipulated or ordered by the court, a party must, without awaiting a discovery request, provide to the other parties: (i) the name and, if known, the address and telephone number of each individual likely to have discoverable information—along with the subjects of that information—that the disclosing party may use to support its claims or defenses, unless the use would be solely for impeachment; (ii) a copy—or a description by category and location—of all documents, electronically stored information, and tangible things that the disclosing party has in its possession, custody, or control and may use to support its claims or defenses, unless the use would be solely for impeachment; (iii) a computation of each category of damages claimed by the disclosing party—who must also make available for inspection and copying as under Rule 34 the documents or other evidentiary material, unless privileged or protected from disclosure, on which each computation is based, including materials bearing on the nature and extent of injuries suffered; and (iv) for inspection and copying as under Rule 34 , any insurance agreement under which an insurance business may be liable to satisfy all or part of a possible judgment in the action or to indemnify or reimburse for payments made to satisfy the judgment. (B) Proceedings Exempt from Initial Disclosure. The following proceedings are exempt from initial disclosure: (i) an action for review on an administrative record; (ii) a forfeiture action in rem arising from a federal statute; (iii) a petition for habeas corpus or any other proceeding to challenge a criminal convictio
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Federal Rules of Civil Procedure Skip to main content Official websites use .gov A .gov website belongs to an official government organization in the United States. Secure .gov websites use HTTPS A lock ( Lock Locked padlock icon ) or https:// means you’ve safely connected to the .gov website. Share sensitive information only on official, secure websites. Federal Rules of Civil Procedure The Federal Rules of Civil Procedure govern civil proceedings in the United States district courts. The purpose of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure is "to secure the just, speedy, and inexpensive determination of every action and proceeding." Fed. R. Civ. P. 1. The rules were first adopted by order of the Supreme Court on December 20, 1937, transmitted to Congress on January 3, 1938, and effective September 16, 1938. The Civil Rules were last amended in 2024. perturbation.justification: The modified text changes 'prompt notice' to 'reasonable attempts to notify'. The original text ensures the other party has sufficient time to seek a protective order or waive compliance. The changed text, by only requiring 'reasonable attempts,' weakens this obligation. The Law Explanation states that FRCP 26 outlines the obligation to respond completely and truthfully to discovery requests and that the 'reasonable attempts' language could obstruct the other party's ability to protect confidential information. Scraped Law Snippet 1 mentions 'Duty to Disclose; General Provisions Governing Discovery' and that 'a party must, without awaiting a discovery request, provide to the other parties' information. The change undermines the ability of a party to seek a protective order because 'reasonable attempts' does not guarantee timely notice, creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Notwithstanding the return or destruction of the Evaluation Material, each party and its Representatives will continue to be bound by its obligations of confidentiality, non-use and other obligations hereunder for a period of three (3) years from the date of this letter agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Notwithstanding the return or destruction of the Evaluation Material, each party and its Representatives will continue to be bound by its obligations of confidentiality, non-use and other obligations hereunder until the Transaction is complete.
perturbation.explanation: The original text provides a fixed term of three years for confidentiality obligations. The modified text replaces this with an indefinite period tied to the completion of the Transaction. If the Transaction never completes, the confidentiality obligations theoretically last forever. This could constitute an unreasonable restraint of trade in violation of antitrust laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 1 of the Sherman Act prohibits contracts, combinations, and conspiracies in restraint of trade. Perpetual confidentiality obligations stemming from a business transaction that never completes could be construed as an unreasonable restraint, particularly if it prevents a party from freely competing or engaging in other business activities. The indefinite duration is the key issue, departing from the original, limited term.

Trusts, etc., in restraint of trade illegal; penalty Every contract, combination in the form of trust or otherwise, or conspiracy, in restraint of trade or commerce among the several States, or with foreign nations, is declared to be illegal. Every person who shall make any contract or engage in any combination or conspiracy hereby declared to be illegal shall be
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text, by replacing a fixed three-year confidentiality period with an indefinite period tied to the completion of a transaction, creates a potential unreasonable restraint of trade, contradicting the Sherman Act. Scraped Law Snippet 1, explicitly states that "Every contract, combination in the form of trust or otherwise, or conspiracy, in restraint of trade or commerce among the several States, or with foreign nations, is declared to be illegal." The indefinite confidentiality obligation could be interpreted as such a restraint, especially if the transaction never completes, preventing a party from freely competing. This creates a substantial risk of non-compliance.
